We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
draw the following adjusting journal entries for fair and accurate presentation of the FS; and 
review the accounting entries prepared in CY 2025 and prepare the necessary adjustments for the errors committed in the recording of the application of RPT income and discount.

We recommended that the Management instruct the: 

Municipal Treasurer to cause the immediate transfer of the funds from the TF to GF;

Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts

Municipal Accountant to properly/appropriately classify and record all receipts in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and other concerned municipal officials and employees prioritize the updating of the Municipality’s LRC, pursuant to Section 191 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:

Municipal Treasurer to give due notice to the taxpayers regarding the finality of the resolution and thereafter, declare the ₱2,864,850.86 tax collections free from protest; and

Municipal Accountant to draw necessary adjusting entries to effect the same thereafter.

We recommended that the Management direct the members of the Municipal Planning and Development Council to facilitate the implementation of all PPAs under the tobacco excise tax funds as provided under RA No. 7171 to enable the tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived therefrom. Further, Management should see to it that the projects are feasible and sustainable and conduct regular monitoring to validate progress of the projects so that the objectives of the fund are achieved.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to intensify and maximize the collection of RPT and SET by sending Notice of Delinquency to concerned taxpayers and conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Instruct all municipal officials and employees to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, especially regarding the limitation of cash advances to the allowable transactions specified therein, to prevent any risk of potential misapplication or misuse of local government funds.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Send demand letters to accountable officers and employees who have not liquidated their cash advances within the required timeframe set by COA Circular No. 97-002 and enforce prompt liquidation of these advances; and
Withhold the full salaries and other benefits of personnel with unliquidated cash advances due to their failure to liquidate these advances within the prescribed period as outlined in Paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines for the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 be followed.
Further, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. He is further requested to cooperate with the Audit Team to ensure that issues are thoroughly discussed with the intention at arriving to a feasible solution, and
The Municipal Supply Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer/Municipal Planning and Development Officer, maintains a Local Road Inventory and Road Map and keeps a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Provide loan amortization of the loans acquired by the Municipality. Disclose loan amortization details, such as, but not limited to: (a) schedule of payment; (b) principal amount due; (c) interest due; and (d) carrying amount under Note 8 of the Notes to Financial Statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary disclosures and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and the Manual on NGAS for LGUs by coordinating with other Department Heads to gather information on the above-mentioned deficiencies, including non-financial information that is deemed necessary for users of the financial statements to understand the operations of the LGU.
We further have recommended that the Audit Team be furnished with the revised Notes.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor to:
Reconstitute of the GFPS-Technical Working Group with a duly elected Chairman among the representatives from various LGU offices/departments or committees represented in the GFPS Executive Committee, including a representative from the LCE’s office, members from the private sector, academe and civil society organizations as appropriate as prescribed in Section 4.1.A.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01;
Create the Monitoring and Evaluation System in accordance with guidelines and Require the GAD M&E Team to conduct an evaluation of the outcomes of the LGU’s GAD policies, programs, and projects and submit a GAD Evaluation Report to the DILG Regional Office for the end of the three-year term 2022-2025 to serve as inputs to local policy-making and planning including GAD planning, programming, budgeting and implementation of the current administration; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the LGU GFPS-TWG Chairman, to refrain from charging expenses that do not address gender-related issues. Furthermore, maintain an updated GAD Database to facilitate the identification of gender issues and ensure full implementation of the PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the members of the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive
physical count and inspection of all PPE items, including the Land, Land
Improvements, Infrastructure Assets, Buildings, and Other Structures and
Transportation Equipment, which hugely contributed to the total deficiencies of
P335,535,479.90, and conduct thorough reconciliation of PPE accounts against the
result of inventory-taking;
b. instruct the Municipal Accounting Office and the GSO to update and maintain the
required Property Cards in their custody for easy reference during the conduct of
inventory-taking; and
c. instruct the Inventory Committee and the MGSO to adopt and fully implement the
one-time cleansing procedures of PPE accounts incorporated under COA Circular
No. 2020-006 so as to properly address the missing/unaccounted/undocumented
PPE items that have been in the books for several years, for a valid reporting and
presentation of PPE accounts balances that are verifiable, as to its existence,
condition and accountability, thus, attain fairness of presentation of all PPE
accounts in the Financial Statements of the Municipality at every reporting period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the OIC-Municipal Accountant to
make the necessary adjustments on the reported balances of RPT and SEF Tax
Receivables for CY 2024 by dropping the recorded penalties of P8,373,649.90 from each
book of accounts as such penalties shall be recognized only upon receipt of tax
payments by the taxpayers, so as to reflect only the accurate receivable balances of
real property taxes in the books of the General and Special Education Funds, in
accordance with the provisions of NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the members of the Municipal Planning and Development Council to assess
strictly the viability of the identified PPAs at the planning stage to ensure that only
those projects that are procurement-and-implementation-ready would be given
budget allocation for the optimal and prompt utilization of 20% Development Fund
within the set timelines of the projects’ implementation; and
b. cause the immediate implementation of the projects to be funded by the 20%
Development Fund to address the developmental needs of the residents and to reap
the desirable socio-economic benefits at the earliest according to DBM-DOF- DILG
JMC No. 01

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Certificate of Availability of Funds
(CAF) for the sixteen (16) listed projects, totaling P36,588,869.66, as enumerated in
Annex H, to the Audit Team for verification;
b. Municipal Accountant, upon ensuring that sufficient funds are available, prepare and
issue the Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF) as support documents for every
procurement of infrastructure projects to ensure the propriety of such undertaking;
c. BAC Chairman to strictly ensure that all contract agreements are accompanied by a
valid and acceptable form of Performance Security as prescribed under the RIRR of
RA No. 9184; and
d. Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of supporting documents before
processing payments in order to establish the legality, validity, and propriety of the
disbursement.

a. immediately enforce the full implementation of calamity-related projects at the most
opportune time particularly those intended for the prevention and mitigation of
disaster risk;
b. ensure that disaster preparedness activities specifically addressing the Taal
Volcano eruption are adequately planned and integrated into the LDRRM
Investment Plan, thereby eliminating the need to unnecessarily reserve funds at the
expense of timely implementation of critical disaster preparedness programs; and
c. strictly maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Fund to its programmed activities in
order to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality of a disaster-resilient
community.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. prioritize gathering the necessary sex-disaggregated data and conduct HGDG
attribution for all GAD-attributed activities to ensure their gender responsiveness;
b. to strictly comply with the provisions on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to
ensure that the PPAs indicated in the annual GPB are aligned with it or with the
identified gender issues to be addressed during the term of the LCE’s leadership;
and
c. allocate sufficient budget for the conduct and participation of the GFPS members
in seminars, trainings, and workshops to enhance their technical capacity and
ensure compliance with GAD-related requirements and guidelines set by the
Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) and other oversight agencies.

We recommended that the Local School Board submit the Quarterly and Annual SEF
Utilization Reports for CY 2024, and in succeeding years, to the Sangguniang Bayan,
through appropriate channels, and furnish copies thereof to the Regional Offices of the
DBM and DILG, pursuant to Item 6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 s. 2017, to ensure
transparency and accountability in reporting the allocation and utilization of the Special
Education Fund to the intended users of information.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) and other concerned municipal officials and employees to prioritize the updating of the Municipality's LRC, pursuant to Section 191 of RA No. 7160.

The Municipal Treasurer:
Regularly monitor the issued checks thru the Report of Checks Issued (RCI), and to send a written notice to the payee, at least one month, before a check becomes stale regardless of the amount;
Prepare the issuance of a new check upon the submission of the stale check;
The cancelled check shall be reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the period of cancellation; and
Submit the original disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant, which will serve as his basis in preparing the adjusting journal entries.
The Municipal Accountant to make a necessary adjustment to recognize the expenses already incurred and the obligation of the Municipal Government for these claims:
Prior Period Adjustments	41,000.00
Accounts Payable			40,380.00

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassified accounts and reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected. Further, classify this as current portion of Loans Payable – Domestic in the Statement of Financial Position and disclose it in the Notes to Financial Statements.
Accounts Payable		 4,595,505.01
Loans Payable – Domestic		4,595,505.01
To reclassify the loans to the proper account.
Disclose additional details of the loans such as, but not limited to: (a) loan provider; (b) purpose of the loans; (c) date of availment; (d) maturity dates of the loans; (e) prevailing interest rates; and (f) loan tenor under Note 8 of the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to:
Coordinate with other Department Heads to gather information on the above-mentioned deficiencies noted by the Audit Team including non-financial information that is deemed necessary for users of the financial statements to understand the operations of the LGU.
Provide adequate disclosures and properly present the same in the Notes to the Financial Statements based on the information provided by the concerned Department Heads. If revisions were made, furnish a copy to the Audit Team for compliance.

We reiterated our recommendation that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 be complied with.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and
The Municipal Treasurer/ Property Officer in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to maintain Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card to establish an accurate and reliable records of payments of individual taxpayers.
Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list of taxpayers, showing their names and the amount due and collectible based on the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card to establish an accurate and reliable basis for setting up of receivables for RPT and SET at the beginning of the year, ensuring the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
Municipal Treasurer to ensure an accurate and updated reporting of the list of delinquent real properties.
The Treasury Office to:
Conduct an information drive and tax campaign to raise the awareness of its constituents regarding the advance payment of the real property taxes and to avail the discounts, encouraging taxpayers to pay promptly and improving collection efficiency of the Municipality on RPT/SET.
Continue sending letters to delinquent taxpayers and post notice of delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay of the local government unit concerned. Also, the Municipality may apply the available legal remedies to generate more revenue that may be appropriated for the implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct:
Municipal Treasurer or concerned municipal official to re-visit the Memorandum of Agreement or guidelines between the Source Agency and the Municipal Government; when the purpose of such fund was created has been accomplished, close the said bank account and revert the balances to General Fund or to the National Treasury, as the case maybe; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to rigorously monitor bank accounts of the Municipal Government and to ensure that no bank account will be inactive or dormant.

We reiterated the prior year recommendation that Management conduct regular coordination between the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to discuss the Statement of Receipts and Expenditures, the financial Statements and the Bank Reconciliation Statements, not the Bank Statement alone, and have analysis of the cash and cash equivalents to have a real picture of the financial status of the Municipality in order to prevent budget overdraft and cash deficit.
Likewise, Management shall limit disbursement within the specific purpose for which they are appropriated/ created and prioritized funding and implementation subject to availability of funds.  Before implementation of a certain project, they shall verify if these projects have appropriation and cash backing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin all municipal officers and employees to adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, particularly on limiting granting of cash advance to allowable transactions stated therein. Also, granting of cash advances for the purpose of paying suppliers and/or creditors deprived the Municipal Government of the control/ convenience/ or security of the local government funds which check payments could provide against loss, thief, misuse and misapplication.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Initiate the reconstitution of the GFPS-Technical Working Group with a duly elected Chairman among the representatives from various LGU offices/departments or committees represented in the GFPS Executive Committee, including a representative from the LCE’s office, members from the private sector, academe and civil society organizations as appropriate as prescribed in   Section 4.1.A.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01;
Prepare the Monitoring and Evaluation System in accordance with guidelines and Require the GAD M&E Team to conduct an evaluation of the outcomes of the LGU’s GAD policies, programs, and projects and submit a GAD Evaluation Report to the DILG Regional Office for the end of the three-year term in 2022 to serve as inputs to local policy-making and planning including GAD planning, programming, budgeting and implementation of the current administration; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the LGU GFPS-TWG Chairman to refrain from charging expenses that are not related in addressing gender-related issues. Furthermore, maintain an updated GAD Database to facilitate the identification of gender issues and ensure full implementation of the PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee, together with the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer, to strictly adhere to the RA 9184 and its Revised IRR, GPPB Resolutions and other statutes in implementing Supplementary Feeding Program; and
Initiate accreditation of farmers and fisherfolk cooperatives and enterprises to alleviate their economic status and participate in the government procurement process, considering that this sector was already enjoined in the yearly Supplemental Feeding Program of the government to be the primary source of agricultural and fishery products.
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, particularly on ensuring the cash advance is granted to allowable transactions stated therein to avoid any risk of possible misapplication or misused of local government funds.
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the MSWDO to implement Supplementary Feeding Program as scheduled. A well-planned and timely implementation shall be effected to address malnutrition among daycare children.
Further, MSWDO together with the day care workers to maintain database of undernourished children enrolled in every day care centers to have readily available information in the implementation of the SFP.
We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to transfer the fund balance for Supplemental Feeding Program from General Fund to Trust Fund, and ensure that succeeding receipts of funds from other government agencies be recorded to its proper books of accounts in conformity with Section 309 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that all planned and budgeted projects, programs, and activities for the year under the 20% DF be implemented as included in the APP pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No.1.  Optimal utilization of the fund and implementation of programs/ projects/ activities within the target completion date must be met to maximize the benefits and opportunities that constituents/ beneficiaries could derive therefrom.
We also recommended that the status of implementation of the projects be included in the agenda of Municipal Development Council regular meeting to promote greater transparency and accountability in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Local Finance Committee to submit a supplemental budget to the Sangguniang Bayan to appropriate the Share from Tobacco Excise Tax, and thus enable the immediate implementation of projects for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and HRMO to:
Check amounts to be withheld from the compensation of municipal officials and employees and certified as to its correctness by the Municipal Accountant before remitted to national government agencies;
Remit all taxes withheld, premium contributions, and loan repayment intact on or before the deadline as provided in the rules and regulations set by the BIR, GSIS, PAG-IBIG, and PhilHealth; and
Reconcile the Due to BIR, GSIS, PAG-IBIG, and PhilHealth accounts with the actual amounts withheld based on the filed tax returns, statement of account, and loan amortization schedule from respective agencies, to identify the under and over-remittances incurred from prior years until the current year.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Instruct the General Service Office (GSO) and the Accounting Unit to perform the
preliminary activities of the one-time cleansing and reconcile their records thereof;

b. Create the Inventory Committee for the one-time cleansing responsible for the
conduct of the actual count of the PPEs;

c. Plan/strategize on how would the Municipality apply the one-time cleansing and
incorporate it in the Physical Inventory Plan to be submitted at least 10 calendar days
before the scheduled inventory-taking activities; and

d. Immediately start the application of One-time cleansing of the PPEs.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
and GSO to:

a. Cause the disposal of the unserviceable properties and to derecognize the latter
from the book of accounts; and

b. While pending disposal, disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the carrying
amount of PPE retired from active use and held for disposal.

a. Assess the viability of the identified PPAs in the planning stage to ensure that only
those projects that are procurement and implementation ready will be given budget
allocation; and

b. Ensure the optimal and timely implementation of the projects to be funded by the
20% Development Fund to address the development needs of the residents and to
reap the desirable socio-economic benefits at the earliest according to
DBM-DOF- DILG JMC No. 01.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
MDRRMO to:

a. Commence the procurement and implementation of calamity-related projects at the
most opportune time particularly those intended for the prevention and mitigation
of disaster risk;

b. Program under the plan response, rehabilitation, and recovery activities specific
for the Taal Volcano eruption without compromising the needs for disaster
prevention and mitigation, and preparedness;

c. Submit the Municipality’s plan for and status of the implementation of the
unutilized balance of LDRRMF amounting to P19,242,666.76; and

d. Judiciously plan, on ensuing periods, on how to maximize the utilization of the
LDRRM Fund and to accelerate its implementation in order to achieve the
goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster risk reduction and management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Evaluate the necessity of appropriating Confidential Funds to the Annual Budget
considering the continual non-utilization of such for an extended period of time;

and

b. If found necessary, assign a Project Accountable Officer to focus on the
preparation of the Physical and Financial Plan indicating the proposed amount
allocated for each program to be submitted to the Municipal Budget Officer as
support to the allocations of Confidential Funds pursuant to Section 4.2 of COA-
DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer
(MBO) to appropriate funds for property insurance of the Municipality subject to its
financial capacity and the General Service Officer (GSO) Designate to prepare the PIF
and cause the appraisal of the properties and submit them for application of insurance
with GSIS to protect the Management thru indemnification in cases of damage or
losses due to fortuitous events particularly the imminent devastation of the Taal
Volcano eruption.

with the provisions on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to ensure that the PPAs
indicated in the annual GPB are aligned with it or with the identified gender issues to
be addressed during the three-year term of the LCE’s leadership.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) coordinate the efforts of public and private sectors and implement the basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment and public health as required under RA No. 9003. observations.recommendation:
1.8. We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. reclassify the above completed projects still included in the CIP
account to the specific PPE accounts and provide for the appropriate
depreciation for each PPE account;
b. stop the practice of recording those which were just issued Notices of
Award at year-end as CIP because the recognition criteria set forth in
IPSAS 1 were not met; and
c. make the necessary journal entries to correct the overstatement of the
CIP account and Accounts Payable account.

2.6. We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. record all grants and donations whether in cash or in kind from other
government agencies and instrumentalities in the books in the period
the transactions occurred; and
b. record the necessary Journal Entries to recognize the donated
equipment from the DENR - EMB and record the corresponding
depreciation in order to present fairly the financial statements.

3.16. We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer in the verification of the
details of the transactions obligated in order to determine whether
these are already consummated obligations. After determining that
these are in fact consummated obligations, require the needed
supporting documents such as CAFOA/Obligation Requests, delivery
receipts, billings, etc. in order for these to be recorded in the
General/Subsidiary Ledger for Accounts Payable through the use of
JEV;
b. make the necessary Journal Entry to cancel the obligation for
terminal leave benefits as the criteria for recognition set forth in
IPSAS were not met;
c. see to it that all payments are duly supported with the necessary
appropriation;
d. set up an effective internal control system in the processing and
payment of Accounts Payable in order to avoid discrepancies in the
settlement of previously recorded payables; and
e. provide a complete detail of the 2022 beginning balance of accounts
payable account in order to ascertain the reliability and completeness
of the account.

4.7. We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to cancel the obligated ₱2,600,062.85 and
declare the same as savings which may be re-appropriated in the
succeeding calendar year since no consummated obligation yet was
incurred;
b. Municipal Accountant to review all supporting documents attached to
every JEV and determine whether the recognition criteria for assets,
liabilities, revenues and expenses are met before these are recorded in
order to avoid the recognition of blind obligations/transactions; and
c. Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to
correct the error in the recognition of unconsummated terminal leave
benefits amounting to ₱2,600,062.85.

5.6. We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. religiously follow COA Circular 2015-009 or the Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs in recognizing financial transactions; and
b. prepare the necessary journal entries to correct the erroneous
recording of the collection of advance payment of real property taxes
(Basic and SEF) in order to comply with paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and
paragraph 46 of IPSAS 3.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to evaluate the causes of the
material discrepancy of P199,459,293.20, to ascertain the fairness and
reliability of the PPE balances in the financial statements; and
b. Municipal GSO, together with the Inventory Committee, to adopt the
guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of
complete physical count of the properties and reconciliation of the results
thereof with accounting records and afterwards submit the same to the
Audit Team to ascertain the existence, validity and correctness of the PPE,
as well as the reliability of balances presented in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal GSO to:
a) prepare the RPCPPE in conformity with the format and contents prescribed
by Annex 49 Chapter II of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II and ensure
that the same be submitted to the Auditor concerned on or before January
31 of each year; and
b) completely fill up and include the vital information on the RPCPPE
pertaining the actual result of the physical inventory count undertaken for
the reliable comparison of the report with the records of the Accounting
office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairman of the
Committee on Disposal to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of those
properties in accordance with afore-cited provisions in order not to expose
those properties to elements that cause further deterioration or loss of salvaged
value and to generate additional income thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
the Acting Municipal Treasurer to determine the cause of discrepancies of their
respective reports and prepare the necessary adjustments for a fair presentation
of the financial statements. Ensure that the accounts of the agency shall be kept
in such detail as necessary and observe consistency in keeping of accounts to
safeguard against inaccurate or misleading information pursuant to Section 111
of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that DRRM funds received from National Government and from other LGUs
be treated as special trust liability recorded under Trust Liabilities – DRRMF in the
Trust Fund books instead of Due to Other NGAs account. Prepare the necessary
accounting entry to recognize the adjustment/transfer of the P484,000.00 from Due
to Other NGAs to Trust Liabilities – DRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer to prepare the list of unreleased checks every
year-end in time for the closing of the books and submit to the Municipal
Accountant;
b. Municipal Accountant to restore to the Cash in Bank and proper liability
accounts the amount corresponding to the unreleased checks at year-end;
and
c. Municipal Accountant to adjust all valid reconciling items determined during
the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements such as stale
checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
facilitate the return of the remaining unutilized/unused fund to the source agency
and ensure that all subsequent unused balance of funds received from any
national government agencies shall be return immediately after the completion of
the project pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the BAC, thru its Secretariat, to
ensure compliance with:
a. Section 12.2 of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Resolution No. 39-2017 dated
December 21, 2017 in the preparation of PMR and APCPI Results; and
b. GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020 in the posting and submission
requirements of the APP, PMR, and APCI to promote transparency in the
government procurement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Development Council, Municipal Planning and
Development Office, Municipal Engineering Office and other offices
concerned in the implementation of projects under the 20 per cent DF to
monitor and evaluate the implementation of all 20 per cent DF
programs/projects/activities to achieve the benefits fully desired to be
derived therefrom in compliance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; and
b. require the Municipal Budget Officer in coordination with the Municipal
Engineer to review the unexpended balances and unobligated appropriations
of PPAs carried over as CY 2021 Continuing Appropriations to determine
which are eligible as continuing PPAs pursuant to the provisions of Sections
321 and 322 of RA No. 7160 and thereafter, consider the reversion of these
unobligated appropriations of completed PPAs which are no longer to be
pursued by the Municipality to be used for other potential developmental
projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor coordinate the efforts of public and
private sectors and implement the basic environmental laws on preserving and
protecting the environment and public health as required under RA No. 9003. We
also recommend that the Municipal Mayor encourage the full participation of the
barangays in the implementation of the law.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) direct the MDRRMO to judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of
the LDRRM Funds and to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve
the goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk
reduction and management; and
b) require the MDRRMO to include in the LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be charged
against the unexpended appropriation of previous years or those funds under
the Trust Liability-DRRM and submit the list of PPAs funded or to be funded
from the unexpended prior years DRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) concerned departments to accomplish properly and complete all the details of
the subsequent Purchase Orders for full disclosure of the vital information on
every future transactions;
b) officials and employees concerned to refrain from resorting to procurement and
disbursement through reimbursement basis and comply strictly to the relevant
provisions of RA No. 9184;
c) Municipal Accountant to ensure the full implementation of the perpetual
inventory method in recording and reporting of all procured supplies and
materials pursuant to the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
d) concerned departments to submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting
documents for further evaluation of the Team.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. Reclassify the above completed projects still included in the CIP
account to the specific PPE accounts and provide for the
appropriate depreciation for each PPE account;
b. Stop the practice of recording as CIP the unfinished portion of on-
going projects and those which were just issued Notices of Award
because the recognition criteria set forth in IPSAS 1 was not yet
met; and
c. Make the necessary journal entries to correct the overstatement of
the CIP account and Accounts Payable account.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Provide depreciation for all depreciable assets owned by the
municipality pursuant to IPSASs 1 and 17;
b. Coordinate with the GSO to determine the specific date/month when
the asset became available for use and when the rehabilitation and
improvement was done in order to establish the appropriate
depreciation period and to compute for the correct depreciation and
record the appropriate journal entries to present fairly the account;
c. In case the specific dates could no longer be established, conduct
revaluation of these structures including the remaining useful lives;
and
d. Reclassify the cost of the FEM Mini Cultural Center to the Other
Structures account and provide for depreciation on said asset.

We recommended that Management instruct the:
a) Municipal Accountant to present properly the enacted supplemental
budgets in the SCBAA in order to present accurate and complete
financial information to decision makers and disclose in the Notes to
Financial Statements any material differences between the Final Budget
and Actual Amounts in order for decision makers to properly monitor
and evaluate the municipality’s PPAs; and
b) Municipal Budget Officer to maintain detailed records vis-à-vis the
Municipality’s appropriations, allotments, obligations and balances that
could be vouched to specific transactions. Likewise, the SAAOB must be
reconciled with the SCBAA in order to provide consensus among the
records.

We recommended that Management provide a detailed disclosure of its Due from Other Funds, Accounts Payable, Loans Payable – Domestic, Due to NGAs, Due to LGUs, Due to Other Funds and Prior Period Adjustment accounts as required by IPSAS 1 and 3 to achieve the objectives of general purpose financial reporting in the public sector which is to provide information useful for decision making, and to demonstrate the accountability of the entity for the resources entrusted to it by: (a) providing information about the sources, allocation and uses of financial resources; (b) providing information about how the entity financed its activities and met its cash requirements; and (c) providing information that is useful in evaluating the entity’s ability to finance its activities and to meet its liabilities and commitments.

Since the LGU had no General Services Officer, we recommended that Management:
Designate employee/ personnel to be in charge of the physical inventories of the Municipality, considering the materiality and volume of the Welfare Goods for Distribution and Drugs and Medicine for Distribution to prevent wastage, improper, irregular and illegal use.
Prepare inventory stock cards/ property cards or simply inventory tagging for easy determination and facilitate Perpetual Method of Inventory to prevent wastage, obsolescence and expiration.
Require the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to conduct regular reconciliation of physical inventory with the subsidiary ledgers of the accounting unit.

We recommended that Management:
Create Inventory Committee and immediately conduct and complete the physical inventory and warrant full compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the existence, condition and accountability assertion of the Municipality on Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) in the Financial Statements.
Renew the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) of PPEs found in the custody of officials/ employees to re-establish accountability.
Be guided by Section 374 to 377 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code on the accountability provisions of government properties.

We recommended that Management prepare a reconciliation between actual amounts on a comparable basis as presented in SCBAA and in the Statement of Financial Performance for the Year Ended December 31, 2021. Entity discrepancies must be disclosed to the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 1 to achieve the objectives of general-purpose financial reporting in the public sector.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to properly present the adjusted balances of each affected component of net assets/ equity by restating the corresponding prior year balances of the affected accounts shown as corresponding figures for the current year Financial Statements in compliance with the requirements of International Public Sector Accounting Standards No. 3.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to the rules on daily deposit intact of all collections with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day provided in Section 62 (C) the Local Treasury Operations Manual to safeguard government funds at all times Moreover, he shall regularly monitor the amount of all collections and the same amount shall be remitted and deposited on a daily basis.  The amount in the Report of Collections and Deposits shall be posted daily to the Cashbooks to monitor and determine the remaining balance of daily collections due for deposit.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and the Heads of Department concerned to abide with the rules and regulations on the disbursement/ disposition of government funds in conformity with the provision of Section 4 of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 39, Chapter 3 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.  In relation thereto, we recommended that the following specific measures shall be adopted:
Since the Municipal Treasurer is required to deposit intact all his collections, as well as all collections turned over to him by the Collectors/ Tellers, with the authorized depository banks daily or not later than the next banking day pursuant to Item C of Section 62 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual, he shall not allow payment of any expenditure from his collections.
Payments out of the Petty Cash Fund shall only be allowed following the guidelines in Item 1.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 48, Chapter 3 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I, to wit:
Cash advance for PCF shall not be used for payment of regular expenses, such as rentals, subscriptions, light and water bills and the like.
Payments out of PCF shall only be allowed for amounts not exceeding P 15,000.00 for each transaction. Splitting of transactions to avoid exceeding the ceiling shall not be allowed.
Payments out of the PCF shall be made only through an approved Petty Cash Voucher which shall be signed by the payee to acknowledge the amount received. The official receipt shall be immediately given by the concerned personnel to the Municipal Treasurer to be attached to the PCV.

We recommended that Management:
Submit a copy of an Ordinance, if any, covering the grant of authority to the Local Chief Executive and/ or the Presiding Officer of the Sanggunian to use savings and augment for the aforesaid expenditures within the same expense class in their respective appropriations under Section 336 of RA No. 7160; and/ or submit an Appropriation Ordinance, if any, covering a supplemental budget authorizing the use of savings under Article 417 of the IRR of RA No. 7160 as amended by AO No. 47, s. 1993 (implementing Section 321 of RA No. 7160) for re-appropriation of object of expenditures (may be to a different expense class) for the aforesaid expenditures
Be judicious and frugal in its expenses for the rest of the year 2021 and onwards considering the application of economy measures to prevent unnecessary and uneconomical disbursement so that expenses are kept within the Approved Budget of the Municipality
Not allowing expenditures which are beyond the Approved Budget to prevent the incurrence of another overdraft in appropriation.  The Municipality shall always be guided by its Approved  Budget and not to spend on items or PPAs not included in the AIP and Approved Budget

We recommended that Management:
Conduct regular coordination between the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer and discuss the Status of Appropriation, Allotment, Obligation and Balances, Statement of Receipts and Expenditures, the financial Statements and the Bank Reconciliation Statements, not the Bank Statement alone, to have a real picture of the financial status of the Municipality in order to prevent budget overdraft and cash deficit.
Management shall limit disbursement within the appropriated budget and prioritize funding and implementation subject to availability of funds.

We recommended that Management thru the Municipal Accountant and LDRRM Officer:
Reconcile their financial records and reports on the DRRM funds;
Maintain a separate Subsidiary Ledger for transfers of the LGU’s unutilized DRRM to the special trust fund to lessen incurrence of errors and
Always disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of the unexpended balance using the required format to establish faithful representation of accounts as mandatory requirement by IPSAS 1.
Moreover, we recommended that Management transfer the unobligated/ unspent appropriation balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Trust Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management require the Local School Board (LSB) Committee to prepare its succeeding budget based on the projects and programs which are allowed and listed in Item 4.0 of the Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department of Interior and Local Government Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017 as amended by DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2, Series of 2020 as basis in the disbursement of funds from the SEF.

We recommended that the Local School Board (LSB) Committee prepare its succeeding budget for SEF and include only those projects and programs which are allowed and listed in Item 4.0 of the Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and Joint Circulars No. 1 and 2, s. 2020 of DepEd, DBM and DILG.
Likewise, the Municipal government is advised to�limit expenditures to those explicitly listed priority expenditures as provided in Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and the aforesaid Joint Circulars of DepEd, DBM and DILG.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to stop from the practice of splitting of contracts. Instead, strictly abide to the provisions of RA 9184 in all procurement activities to promote good governance and adhere to the principle of transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency and economy, to the advantage of the LGU.

We recommended that Management thru the Bids and Awards Committee require the end-user units prepare their respective PPMPs for their different PPAs and submit to the Budget Office for evaluation in order to ensure consistency with the Procuring Entity’s budget proposal.  The PPMPs shall be forwarded to the shall be forwarded to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into an indicative APP, and to the BAC for final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality. These APP and PPMP as part of its Procurement Planning and Budgeting Linkage shalle be in compliance to Sections 7.3.2 and 7.3.4 of Rule II of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.  The APP shall then be submitted to the GPPB as provided in Section 7.3.5 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit all the aforementioned supporting documents to establish validity, regularity and propriety of the claims such as the Official Receipt/ Sales Invoice, Delivery Receipt, duly accomplished Purchase Request and Purchase Order providing the specific details of the meals to be served and the number of pax to be served and pictures showing proof of delivery to the Municipality and the List of Recipients/ Beneficiaries.
For succeeding transactions of the same nature, they shall see to it that supporting documents are complete before certifying the Disbursement Voucher and effecting payment. Strict compliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation be always adhered for all claims against government funds.

We recommended that:
Since government resources are scarce, Overtime work with Overtime Pay should be avoided by Management by adequately planning work activities. The rendition of overtime services shall be authorized only when extremely necessary, such as when a particular work or activity cannot be completed within the regular work hours and that non-completion of the same will: a) cause financial loss to the government or its instrumentalities; b) embarrass the government due to its inability to meet its commitments; or c) negate the purposes for which the work or activity was conceived.
The HRMO shall formulate and adopt internal rules and procedures on the rendition of overtime services of their respective personnel for approval of the Local Chief Executive which shall be circumscribed within the policies and guidelines under the Joint Circular of the DBM and CSC.
In extreme cases whereby the rendition of Overtime work is necessary, the payment in cash of overtime services through Overtime Pay may be authorized only in exceptional cases when the application of CTO for all overtime hours would adversely affect the operations of the agency.  The HRMO in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant shall check first the availability of funds before recommending the grant of Overtime Pay.  The HRMO shall check first all the guidelines pertaining to Overtime provided in CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2015 and the documentary requirements for Overtime with Pay in Item 5.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
In extreme cases whereby the rendition of Overtime work is necessary, the HRMO shall prepare scheduling of staff who will render overtime so as to comply with the limitations on Overtime provided in CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2015 and to give personnel the Right of Weekly Rest Day of not less than twenty-four (24) consecutive hours of rest period after every six (6) consecutive normal work days for health concerns and well-being of personnel.
The HRMO shall check entries in the DTR of personnel such that, all entries in DTRs shall pertain to the direct print-out of the actual personnel attendance from the Biometrics itself , except when the employee is on official business/  assigned in the field where it would be impossible for the personnel to log-in and out thru the biometrics.  A Travel Order/ Locator Slip/ Leave of Absence shall support working days in the DTR with no entries from the biometrics.
The HRMO shall strictly ensure that the immediate supervisor signs the DTRs of personnel.

We recommended that Management enforce regularly that all procurement of goods and services be paid by checks directly to suppliers/ contractors/ service providers except for petty disbursements out of petty cash fund in pursuance of COA Circular No. 97-002 and to comply with BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23 ���- 2014 to avoid liabilities and penalties arising from non-compliance as clearly stipulated in the same BIR Revenue Memorandum Order.

We recommended that the Management stop the practice of charging procured PPE items to MOOE contrary to Section 4 of PD No. 1445, Section 336 of the Local Government Code and Budget Operations Manual for LGUs.  The needed PPE items of all Offices/ Departments/ Divisions as well as projects and programs under the 20% Development Fund and 5% Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund which are development projects with more than one-year life span shall be properly incorporated as CO in the Municipal Annual Budget and the procurement for the said projects shall be charged against the appropriation for CO instead of MOOE pursuant to the aforementioned guidelines.
We also recommended Management thru the Local Finance Committee that in their preparation of the Annual Budget and Annual Investment Plan, CO and MOEE are clearly defined and identified in order that juggling in the budget will be prevented.

We recommended that:

observations.recommendation:
We have reiterated the prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor shall:

Create an Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count and prepare the RPCPPE necessary for control and monitoring of PPEs. Incorporate in the RCPPE the whereabouts, serviceability and physical condition, as basis for impairment. In effect, the accuracy, validity, existence and actual condition could be ascertained and                render the account not doubtful.

Require the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of Supply Officer, and the Municipal Accountant to immediately start and regularly maintain their respective property and ledger cards per category of property, plant and equipment to record deliveries of properties in the ensuing year.  Incorporate in their property and ledgers cards the whereabouts, serviceability and physical condition as stated in the RCPPE for reference and verification purposes and

Require the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile the book records with the inventory report as internal control over the PPEs of the municipality.

We have reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. 

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. 

Require the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

Create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System and 

Compliance with Item IN9 of IPSAS No. 1 is strictly recommended.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the APP and PPMP as part of its Procurement Planning and Budgeting Linkage in compliance to Section 7.3.2 and 7.3.4 of Rule II of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.  The APP shall then be submitted to the GPPB as provided in Section 7.3.5.  The APP will serve as a guide for the proper planning of all procurement activities and for sound fiscal management. Likewise, PPMP should be prepared to support the approved APP mentioned in Section 7.3.2 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 to be fully complied, for easy validation of items procured against the approved plan.  Lastly, APP and PPMP is an effective tool in tracking implemented and unimplemented programs, activities and projects.
observations.reason:
Per validation on GPPB website at
https://www.gppb.gov.ph/app.php,
the Municipality was not among those agencies listed as compliant agencies in the submission of APP.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approved by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
